_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Spartanburg County, SC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses.
display_name: Spartanburg County, South Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.fate: Log and frame courthouse. Replaced as county grew.
Brick courthouse. Replaced by larger structure.
Survived the Civil War without destruction. Served for over a century before replacement. Records preserved through successive buildings.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or war damage
record_losses.description: Spartanburg County's courthouse survived the Civil War and has not experienced a catastrophic fire. Records run continuously from the county's 1785 formation (previously part of the old Ninety Six District). This makes Spartanburg among the best-preserved Upcountry counties for genealogical research. The 1903 Pacolet River flood destroyed mill villages but did not affect the courthouse in the city of Spartanburg.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Spartanburg County after 1785, the civil records almost certainly survive. The Pacolet flood of 1903 destroyed homes and lives but not the courthouse — however, personal documents (family Bibles, letters, deeds held privately) that were in the flood zone were lost.
record_losses.source: SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from county formation
deeds.repository: Spartanburg County Register of Deeds; SC Department of Archives and History microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com SC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records survive from near-formation
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose papers from earliest decades are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Spartanburg County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds survive from early period. Marriage licenses required statewide after 1911.
marriages.gaps: Pre-1911 coverage is through bonds and church records rather than licenses.
marriages.repository: Spartanburg County Probate Court; SC Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.repository: SC Department of Archives and History; Spartanburg County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: SC DHEC; SC Department of Archives and History

substitute_sources.description: Spartanburg's massive textile complex — Clifton Manufacturing, Pacolet Manufacturing, Spartan Mills, Beaumont Mill, and dozens of others — generated extensive employment and housing records. Survival is uneven. The Pacolet flood of 1903 destroyed some early Pacolet Manufacturing Company records along with the physical plant and village.
substitute_sources.repository: Spartanburg County Historical Association; Wofford College Archives; SC Department of Archives and History. Some records at the Southern Historical Collection, UNC Chapel Hill.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Where surviving, mill records are the primary documentation for textile worker families who owned no property and appeared in county records only at census time. The Pacolet flood destroyed family possessions as well as company records — personal Bibles, letters, and documents held in flood-zone homes were lost.
substitute_sources.source: Racine, Philip N. Spartanburg County: A Pictorial History. Virginia Beach: Donning Company, 1986.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Spartanburg County is enumerated from the first federal census. Manufacturing schedules (1820–1880) document early industrial operations including iron furnaces.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline. Manufacturing schedules document the iron furnace economy predating textiles. Mill village enumerations after 1880 group workers by village, making it possible to identify which mill a family worked at.

substitute_sources.description: The Southern Railway maintained shops and repair facilities in Spartanburg. Railroad employment records, where they survive, document workers' names, dates of service, and positions.
substitute_sources.repository: Norfolk Southern Corporation Archives (successor); some records at various university collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for families employed by the railroad rather than the mills. Railroad workers were generally better paid and more mobile than mill workers, and their records may document interstate movement.
substitute_sources.source: Herr, Kincaid A. The Louisville and Nashville Railroad, 1850–1963. University Press of Kentucky, 2000.

narrative_hooks: Spartanburg County's courthouse survived every crisis that struck the county — including the 1903 Pacolet flood that destroyed entire mill villages. {ancestor_name}'s civil records are almost certainly there; the question is whether the mill company records that would complete the picture survived the closures.

The Pacolet flood of 1903 destroyed more than lives and buildings — it destroyed the personal documents of every family in the flood zone. If {ancestor_name} lived along the Pacolet, {pronoun_possessive} family Bible, letters, and personal papers may have been lost with the houses.

Before 1911, South Carolina required no marriage license, so a missing marriage record for {ancestor_name} in Spartanburg County is not unusual — church records and family records are the primary substitutes.

If {ancestor_name} worked at one of Spartanburg's dozens of textile mills, the courthouse may hold less about {pronoun_object} than the company's own records — the question is whether those records survived the mill's eventual closure.
source_references: Racine, Philip N. Spartanburg County: A Pictorial History. Virginia Beach: Donning Company, 1986.
SC Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records in the South Carolina State Archives.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: South Carolina County Records. Columbia, 1937–1942.
